source.paper: Get Ahead Neuro EMQ · Page 1 | Theme 7: Interpretation of cerebrospinal fluid results

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is admitted with HEADACHE AND NECK STIFFNESS. A lumbar puncture is performed and reveals the following results: clear fluid, lymphocytes 2/mm3, neutrophils 1/mm3, LOW GLUCOSE, high protein, POSITIVE INDIA INK STAIN.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: India ink stain demonstrates the encapsulated yeast Cryptococcus neoformans. The low glucose with a lymphocyte-predominant, low cell-count CSF and high protein fits chronic cryptococcal meningitis, classically in immunocompromised/HIV patients.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A near-acellular CSF with normal glucose/protein but OLIGOCLONAL BANDS (IgG not present in serum) is the hallmark of multiple sclerosis. The clinical framing is incidental; the discriminator is the oligoclonal bands.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: A lymphocyte-predominant CSF with LOW glucose, high protein and a POSITIVE ZIEHL-NEELSEN (acid-fast) stain is tuberculous meningitis. TB is the classic cause of a lymphocytic CSF with low glucose.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A neutrophil-predominant, cloudy CSF with LOW glucose, high protein and GRAM-NEGATIVE DIPLOCOCCI is bacterial meningitis due to Neisseria meningitidis (meningococcus).

questions.keyPoint: India ink positive CSF = Cryptococcus neoformans, typically in immunocompromised/HIV.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is admitted with HEADACHE AND NECK STIFFNESS. A lumbar puncture is performed and reveals the following results: clear fluid, LYMPHOCYTES 340/mm3, neutrophils 3/mm3, NORMAL GLUCOSE, normal protein, NO ORGANISMS SEEN.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: India ink stain demonstrates the encapsulated yeast Cryptococcus neoformans. The low glucose with a lymphocyte-predominant, low cell-count CSF and high protein fits chronic cryptococcal meningitis, classically in immunocompromised/HIV patients.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A near-acellular CSF with normal glucose/protein but OLIGOCLONAL BANDS (IgG not present in serum) is the hallmark of multiple sclerosis. The clinical framing is incidental; the discriminator is the oligoclonal bands.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Lymphocyte-predominant CSF with NORMAL glucose, normal protein and NO organisms is the classic viral meningitis picture; mumps is a typical cause. Glucose stays normal because viruses do not consume it as bacteria/TB/fungi do.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: A lymphocyte-predominant CSF with LOW glucose, high protein and a POSITIVE ZIEHL-NEELSEN (acid-fast) stain is tuberculous meningitis. TB is the classic cause of a lymphocytic CSF with low glucose.

questions.keyPoint: Lymphocytes with NORMAL glucose and no organisms = viral meningitis (e.g. mumps).

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a SUDDEN-ONSET SEVERE OCCIPITAL HEADACHE. It started an hour ago and is accompanied by VOMITING. He has a history of POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASE. On examination, he has a STIFF NECK.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Severe, strictly unilateral headaches occurring at the same time each day in a cluster, lasting under an hour, with ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, conjunctival redness) are cluster headaches.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Giant cell arteritis: elderly, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, raised ESR.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Recurrent unilateral throbbing headache with vomiting and a visual aura (flashing lights) is migraine.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Sudden-onset (thunderclap) occipital headache with vomiting and neck stiffness is subarachnoid haemorrhage. Polycystic kidney disease is associated with berry aneurysms, the usual cause of spontaneous SAH.

questions.keyPoint: Thunderclap occipital headache + PCKD (berry aneurysm) = subarachnoid haemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 41-year-old man presents with a 2-WEEK HISTORY of BLOODY DIARRHOEA and LOWER LIMB WEAKNESS. He denies any other symptoms. On examination, there is BILATERAL WEAKNESS IN THE DISTAL LIMBS, with ABSENT DEEP TENDON REFLEXES and diminished sensation. There is no abnormality in the upper limbs or cranial nerves.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Acute unilateral LMN facial weakness affecting the WHOLE half of the face (unable to frown - forehead involved) with preauricular pain and intact facial sensation is Bell's palsy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Ascending limb weakness with AREFLEXIA following a diarrhoeal illness (Campylobacter) is Guillain-Barre syndrome, an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Coexisting LMN signs (marked wasting) and UMN signs (brisk reflexes) in the same limbs, with NO sensory involvement, is the hallmark of motor neurone disease.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: FATIGABLE weakness - diplopia, ptosis and grip that weakens with repetition (worse after exertion) - with preserved sensation and reflexes is myasthenia gravis.

questions.keyPoint: Ascending areflexic weakness after diarrhoea = Guillain-Barre syndrome.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with PAIN IN HER LEFT EAR. When she looked in the mirror, she noticed that the LEFT SIDE OF HER FACE WAS DROOPING. These symptoms occurred 2 days ago, but have not improved. On examination, the left side of her face is weak and she is NOT ABLE TO SMILE OR FROWN adequately. SENSATION TO THE FACE IS UNAFFECTED.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Acute unilateral LMN facial weakness affecting the WHOLE half of the face (unable to frown - forehead involved) with preauricular pain and intact facial sensation is Bell's palsy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Ascending limb weakness with AREFLEXIA following a diarrhoeal illness (Campylobacter) is Guillain-Barre syndrome, an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Proximal weakness that IMPROVES with repeated use, in a smoker with haemoptysis and weight loss (small-cell lung cancer), is Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome - a paraneoplastic disorder of presynaptic calcium channels.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: FATIGABLE weakness - diplopia, ptosis and grip that weakens with repetition (worse after exertion) - with preserved sensation and reflexes is myasthenia gravis.

questions.keyPoint: Unilateral LMN facial weakness involving the forehead = Bell's palsy (LMN VII).

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with a SUDDEN PAIN BEHIND HER RIGHT EYE associated with BLURRED VISION. Last month, she experienced sudden LOSS OF USE OF HER LEFT ARM but this resolved spontaneously within 48 hours. On examination, there was NO limb weakness or sensory loss.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Distal lower-limb wasting with a HIGH-ARCHED foot (pes cavus) and a length-dependent sensory loss in a young adult is Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Simultaneous involvement of separate named nerves (right median - first three digits; left common peroneal - foot drop) in a diabetic is mononeuritis multiplex.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Painful visual loss (optic neuritis) plus a previous transient arm weakness that resolved indicates CNS lesions disseminated in time and space - multiple sclerosis.

choices.label: M
choices.explanation: A floppy, areflexic infant with progressive weakness and feeding difficulty but a normal alert mental state (preserved cognition) is spinal muscular atrophy (anterior horn cell degeneration).

questions.keyPoint: Optic neuritis + earlier transient deficit = MS (disseminated in time and space).

questions.stem: A 29-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being involved in a FIGHT. He is clearly UNDER THE INFLUENCE OF ALCOHOL and is aggressive towards staff. It is difficult to understand what he is saying, as the words are coming out SLURRED AND MUDDLED. He UNDERSTANDS what the doctor is saying to him, and provides appropriate answers.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman has developed what she calls 'SLEEPING ATTACKS'. These can come on at any time of day and last around 10 minutes. She is worried that they are interfering with her job in the stock market.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Recurrent irresistible daytime sleep attacks are narcolepsy; daytime sleepiness is treated with stimulants such as amphetamines (or modafinil).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Tremor, sweating, confusion, autonomic arousal and hallucinations a couple of days after admission (forced alcohol abstinence) is delirium tremens - treat with a reducing regimen of chlordiazepoxide.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Insidious progressive memory loss with impaired MMSE and otherwise normal examination is Alzheimer's dementia; donepezil (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) is used for mild-to-moderate disease.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Small, irregular, unequal pupils that accommodate but DO NOT react to light (Argyll Robertson pupils) with painless ulcers (gummata) indicate tertiary/neurosyphilis - treat with PENICILLIN.

questions.keyPoint: Narcolepsy (daytime sleep attacks) is treated with stimulants (amphetamines/modafinil).

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the GP with WEAKNESS IN HIS LEFT LEG. This started 2 DAYS ago and was PRECEDED BY A FLU-LIKE ILLNESS. On examination, the LEFT LEG is markedly WEAKER than the right. There is NO evidence of cognitive impairment.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Headache, fever, drowsiness with cognitive impairment and a seizure points to a focal intracranial infection - a cerebral abscess (or encephalitis); the file marks cerebral abscess.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Acute headache and vomiting with neck stiffness (pain on neck flexion) and no focal/cognitive deficit is meningitis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Acute ASYMMETRIC flaccid limb weakness following a febrile/flu-like prodrome, with preserved cognition, is poliomyelitis (anterior horn cell infection).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Progressive cognitive and behavioural decline in a child YEARS AFTER MEASLES is subacute sclerosing panencephalitis, a late complication of measles virus infection.

questions.keyPoint: Asymmetric flaccid paralysis after a flu-like illness = poliomyelitis.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the GP with a 2-MONTH HISTORY of worsening headaches. The headaches are WORSE IN THE MORNING and are EXACERBATED BY COUGHING AND LAUGHING. More recently, she has been complaining of BLURRED VISION and NAUSEA.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: An elderly woman with unilateral headache, SCALP TENDERNESS and shoulder girdle pain (associated polymyalgia rheumatica) has giant cell (temporal) arteritis - a medical emergency due to the risk of blindness.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Headache with raised-ICP signs (papilloedema) and a seizure in a young woman on the OCP (a prothrombotic risk factor) is cerebral venous sinus (sagittal sinus) thrombosis.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Facial pain over the cheek (maxillary sinus), worse on coughing/bending, following a recent cold (coryza), with local tenderness, is sinusitis.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Progressive headache, worse in the morning and on coughing/straining, with nausea and visual blurring, is the classic raised-intracranial-pressure picture of a space-occupying lesion.

questions.keyPoint: Morning headache worse on coughing + visual change = raised ICP from a space-occupying lesion.

questions.stem: This nerve supplies the MUSCLES OF THE TONGUE.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The abducens nerve (CN VI) supplies lateral rectus, which abducts (moves laterally) the eye.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The accessory nerve (CN XI) supplies sternocleidomastoid and trapezius.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) is the motor supply to the muscles of the tongue.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: The trigeminal nerve (CN V, mandibular division) supplies the muscles of mastication.

questions.keyPoint: Tongue movement = hypoglossal (XII).

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy is playing football at school when he suddenly FALLS TO THE FLOOR. Soon after, his friends notice HIM JERKING around and unresponsive. During the episode, he is INCONTINENT OF URINE and starts BLEEDING FROM HIS MOUTH. When the boy finally regains consciousness, he is CONFUSED AND SLEEPY.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Clinically a strong contender (impaired awareness + lip-smacking automatisms lasting minutes), but it is NOT the letter marked in the file (G was marked).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Marked answer and clinically correct: a child aged 6 months-5 years with a generalised seizure provoked by a febrile illness (here otitis media), no prior epilepsy, lasting <15 minutes (simple febrile convulsion).

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Clinically the correct answer (continuous/recurrent seizures >5 min without regaining consciousness), but it is NOT the letter marked in the file (B was marked).

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: CORRECT. A teenager who suddenly FALLS with generalised JERKING, unresponsiveness, urinary INCONTINENCE, TONGUE-BITING and POST-ICTAL drowsiness is describing a generalised TONIC-CLONIC seizure: abrupt loss of consciousness with a tonic (stiffening) phase then a clonic (rhythmic jerking) phase, followed by a confusional/drowsy post-ictal period. Lateral tongue-biting and incontinence strongly favour a true epileptic GTC over syncope or a non-epileptic attack.

questions.keyPoint: Lateral tongue-biting + urinary incontinence + post-ictal drowsiness point to a true generalised tonic-clonic seizure, not syncope or a non-epileptic attack.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman has a long history of SHAKING IN HER HANDS. She finds that it is WORSE WITH WRITING, and this is interfering with her job. ALCOHOL RELIEVES THE TREMOR, and she is worried that she may DEVELOP ALCOHOL DEPENDENCE.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: irregular, jerky, non-repetitive movements flitting between limbs; with a strong autosomal-DOMINANT FAMILY HISTORY (father died young of movement disorder) this indicates HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: sustained involuntary muscle contraction causing abnormal posturing. Spasmodic torticollis (cervical dystonia) produces a turning/twisting of the neck, here on the right.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: bilateral postural/action tremor worse with movement (writing), often familial, classically RELIEVED BY ALCOHOL, and improved by beta-blockers/primidone.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: a coarse, large-amplitude tremor present at rest, on posture and on action, caused by midbrain/red nucleus lesions (e.g. multiple sclerosis); it is severely disabling, causing dropping/spilling of objects.

questions.keyPoint: Essential tremor: bilateral postural/action tremor, worse on activity, FAMILIAL, RELIEVED BY ALCOHOL; treat with propranolol or primidone.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman complains of a 6-WEEK HISTORY OF WORSENING HEADACHES. This morning, she noticed that she was occasionally GETTING DOUBLE VISION, which was most noticeable WHEN SHE LOOKED TOWARDS THE LEFT SIDE.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: the abducens (VI) nerve supplies lateral rectus. A VI palsy causes failure of abduction and horizontal diplopia that is WORSE ON LOOKING TOWARDS THE AFFECTED (left) SIDE. The long intracranial course makes it a sensitive 'false-localising' sign of raised intracranial pressure (here, worsening headaches).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: loss of the temporal fields of both eyes (BITEMPORAL HEMIANOPIA) localises to the optic chiasm, compressed here by a pituitary tumour. The acromegalic features (growth of chin, nose, hands) and morning headaches indicate a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: loss of the temporal field of the right eye plus the nasal field of the left eye is a LEFT HOMONYMOUS HEMIANOPIA, localising to the right optic tract (lesion behind the chiasm, before the geniculate).

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: a superior homonymous quadrantanopia ('PIE IN THE SKY') results from interruption of Meyer's loop in the temporal lobe optic radiation, here on the left causing loss of the upper right quadrants of both eyes.

questions.keyPoint: Sixth (abducens) nerve palsy = horizontal diplopia worse on looking toward the lesion; a false-localising sign of raised ICP.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a short HISTORY OF BLURRED VISION. He has NO OTHER SYMPTOMS. On examination, his RIGHT EYE IS LOOKING DOWNWARDS AND LATERALLY, and he is UNABLE TO MOVE IT from this position. The PUPIL ON THE RIGHT SIDE IS SMALLER THAN THAT ON THE LEFT.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: the abducens (VI) nerve supplies lateral rectus. A VI palsy causes failure of abduction and horizontal diplopia that is WORSE ON LOOKING TOWARDS THE AFFECTED (left) SIDE. The long intracranial course makes it a sensitive 'false-localising' sign of raised intracranial pressure (here, worsening headaches).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: a third (oculomotor) nerve palsy produces a 'DOWN-AND-OUT' eye (unopposed lateral rectus and superior oblique) with restricted movement and ptosis. The vignette notes a SMALLER right pupil rather than a dilated one; a pupil-sparing (medical) III palsy is classic, so the smaller pupil wording is internally inconsistent with the typical surgical (mydriatic) picture, but the eye position is diagnostic of a III palsy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: loss of the temporal field of the right eye plus the nasal field of the left eye is a LEFT HOMONYMOUS HEMIANOPIA, localising to the right optic tract (lesion behind the chiasm, before the geniculate).

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: a superior homonymous quadrantanopia ('PIE IN THE SKY') results from interruption of Meyer's loop in the temporal lobe optic radiation, here on the left causing loss of the upper right quadrants of both eyes.

questions.keyPoint: Third (oculomotor) nerve palsy = 'down-and-out' eye + ptosis +/- dilated pupil; a fixed dilated pupil suggests a compressive (surgical) cause e.g. posterior communicating artery aneurysm.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man has a 6-MONTH HISTORY OF TREMOR that occurs at rest. He finds it especially DIFFICULT TO PERFORM FINE MOVEMENTS with his hands, such as writing. On examination, his RIGHT ARM IS RIGID.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: sudden focal neurological deficit (facial weakness, dysarthria) with a CT excluding haemorrhage indicates an ISCHAEMIC STROKE; aspirin 300 mg is given once haemorrhage is excluded (after considering thrombolysis if within the window).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: headache, vomiting, neck stiffness and a NON-BLANCHING (petechial/purpuric) rash indicate MENINGOCOCCAL MENINGITIS/SEPTICAEMIA; immediate IM/IV benzylpenicillin is the recommended emergency treatment (especially pre-hospital).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: unilateral, paroxysmal, stabbing/burning facial pain triggered by touching the area is TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA; carbamazepine is the first-line treatment.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: the triad of resting tremor, bradykinesia (difficulty with fine movements) and asymmetric rigidity is PARKINSON'S DISEASE; levodopa (with a dopa-decarboxylase inhibitor) is the most effective symptomatic treatment, especially when symptoms are disabling.

questions.keyPoint: Parkinson's disease (resting tremor, bradykinesia, asymmetric rigidity): levodopa + carbidopa/benserazide is the most effective symptomatic drug.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman faints during her DAUGHTER'S SCHOOL PLAY. Her husband says that she TURNED SLIGHTLY PALE and SLUMPED OVER while still seated. She did NOT JERK or lose urinary continence. She denies CHEST PAIN, SHORTNESS OF BREATH OR PALPITATIONS. She RECOVERED SPONTANEOUSLY AFTER 30 SECONDS and now feels back to her usual self.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: collapse/dizziness provoked by NECK EXTENSION AND HEAD-TURNING (reaching up to peg washing) in an elderly patient, without full loss of consciousness, is classic of CAROTID SINUS HYPERSENSITIVITY, where pressure on the carotid sinus triggers bradycardia/hypotension.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: syncope occurring DURING OR IMMEDIATELY AFTER PASSING URINE (classically a man standing at night) is MICTURITION SYNCOPE, a situational reflex (vagally mediated) faint with brief LOC and quick recovery.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: recurrent syncope specifically on STANDING UP FROM LYING OR SITTING, with no cardiac symptoms, is ORTHOSTATIC (POSTURAL) HYPOTENSION due to a fall in blood pressure on standing.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: a situational/emotional faint with prodromal PALLOR, brief loss of consciousness, no incontinence or jerking, and rapid spontaneous recovery is a classic VASOVAGAL (neurocardiogenic) SYNCOPE.

questions.keyPoint: Vasovagal syncope: emotional/situational trigger, prodromal pallor/nausea, brief LOC, rapid full recovery, no post-ictal phase.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman presents with a 10-HOUR HISTORY OF SEVERE HEADACHE AND VOMITING. She was previously well. Examination reveals PAIN ON NECK FLEXION. In the emergency department, she receives appropriate INITIAL MANAGEMENT.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Marked answer: with acute severe headache, vomiting and neck stiffness, a CT HEAD is performed first to exclude a space-occupying lesion or raised intracranial pressure (and subarachnoid haemorrhage) BEFORE a lumbar puncture is done to confirm meningitis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: a thunderclap headache ('hit with a brick') with a NORMAL CT still requires a LUMBAR PUNCTURE (ideally >12 hours after onset) to look for XANTHOCHROMIA, confirming a SUBARACHNOID HAEMORRHAGE that CT has missed.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: neurological deficits separated in TIME (right leg last year, left hand now) and SPACE in a young woman suggest MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS; MRI of the brain (and cord) is the investigation of choice to demonstrate demyelinating plaques.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: FATIGABLE weakness (diplopia, ptosis, grip weakening on repetition, worse after exercise) is MYASTHENIA GRAVIS; the Tensilon (edrophonium) test transiently improves strength and supports the diagnosis (though anti-AChR antibodies/single-fibre EMG are now used in practice).

questions.keyPoint: Suspected meningitis with headache + meningism: after initial treatment, do a CT head first if any concern for raised ICP, then LP to confirm the diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman complains of a WEAKNESS IN HER LEFT HAND that has developed over the LAST FEW HOURS. She is otherwise well and has NO OTHER SYMPTOMS. LAST YEAR, she had a SIMILAR WEAKNESS IN HER RIGHT LEG, which lasted for 7 days.

questions.keyPoint: Thunderclap headache with a normal CT: do a lumbar puncture (>12 h after onset) to detect xanthochromia and confirm/exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man complains of a 12-MONTH HISTORY OF WORSENING VISION. He says that his vision is becoming MORE BLURRY and that he is finding it especially DIFFICULT FOCUSING WHEN READING THE NEWSPAPER. FUNDOSCOPY IS UNREMARKABLE.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: TRANSIENT, painless MONOCULAR visual loss (like a curtain descending) that RESOLVES SPONTANEOUSLY is AMAUROSIS FUGAX, usually due to retinal artery emboli (carotid disease); it is a form of TIA warranting urgent vascular workup.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: gradual visual deterioration with GLARE FROM HEADLIGHTS at night and a REDUCED RED REFLEX BILATERALLY is classic of CATARACT (lens opacification); treated with phacoemulsification and lens implant.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: a discrete area of visual loss (a 'dark patch') in the centre of the field of ONE EYE with the rest of vision preserved is a CENTRAL SCOTOMA, typically from optic nerve (e.g. optic neuritis - fitting a young woman) or macular pathology.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: long-sightedness (hypermetropia/presbyopia) causes difficulty focusing on NEAR objects (e.g. reading the newspaper) with a NORMAL FUNDUS; it is a refractive error correctable with convex lenses.

questions.keyPoint: Difficulty focusing on near objects with a normal fundus = hypermetropia/presbyopia (long-sightedness), corrected with convex lenses.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman attends the dermatology outpatient clinic with MULTIPLE SKIN LESIONS on her body. On examination, she has EIGHT LIGHT-BROWN MACULES on her arms and trunk, and FOUR FIRM RUBBERY LESIONS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: progressive CEREBELLAR ATAXIA in a young child (difficulty walking, clumsiness) with TELANGIECTASIA (thin red spidery vessels, classically on the face/conjunctivae) is ATAXIA TELANGIECTASIA, an autosomal-recessive disorder (ATM gene) with immunodeficiency and cancer risk.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: multiple CAFE-AU-LAIT MACULES (>=6, >15 mm) plus cutaneous NEUROFIBROMAS (firm rubbery lesions) are diagnostic features of NF1 (von Recklinghausen's disease), an autosomal-dominant neurocutaneous disorder.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: a PARKINSONIAN rest tremor combined with prominent AUTONOMIC FAILURE (postural dizziness from orthostatic hypotension, urinary incontinence) is SHY-DRAGER SYNDROME, a form of MULTIPLE SYSTEM ATROPHY (MSA).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Marked answer and correct: a central cervical cord cavity (syrinx) first interrupts the decussating spinothalamic fibres, causing DISSOCIATED SENSORY LOSS - loss of PAIN AND TEMPERATURE in a CAPE/UPPER-LIMB distribution with PRESERVED JOINT POSITION SENSE (dorsal columns).

questions.keyPoint: NF1: >=6 cafe-au-lait macules + neurofibromas (also axillary freckling, Lisch nodules); autosomal dominant, chromosome 17.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman has a 2-MONTH HISTORY OF SHOULDER PAIN associated with SENSORY LOSS IN HER ARMS. On examination, you note that she is UNABLE TO FEEL PAIN AND TEMPERATURE IN BOTH HANDS, although her JOINT POSITION SENSE IS INTACT. There is NO OTHER MOTOR OR SENSORY LOSS.

On further questioning, he admits to having PAROXYSMS OF ANXIETY, SWEATING AND PALPITATIONS that are brought on BY STRESS, which he describes as 'PANIC ATTACKS'. He has a PAST MEDICAL HISTORY OF KIDNEY CANCER and 'TUMOURS IN HIS EYES'.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old woman is having difficulty walking and has had MULTIPLE FALLS recently. On gait examination she is walking VERY SLOWLY and is CAREFULLY WATCHING AND PLACING HER FEET. On examination there is NO WEAKNESS OR SENSORY DEFICIT.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Antalgic gait is a painful, protective limp with shortened stance on the affected side - expected in the early period after a hip replacement, with no neurological deficit.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Gait apraxia (frontal/NPH gait) is exactly this picture: slow, cautious, magnetic 'glued-to-floor' walking with falls despite normal power and sensation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: High-stepping gait results from foot drop; ascending weakness, areflexia and bilateral ankle weakness (Guillain-Barre) cause bilateral foot drop and a high-stepping, tripping gait.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Waddling gait is caused by a proximal myopathy; chronic STEROID use causes a proximal (steroid) myopathy giving difficulty rising from a chair and a waddling gait.

questions.keyPoint: A slow, cautious gait with falls but normal power and sensation, where the patient watches the feet, is classically gait apraxia (frontal lobe / normal pressure hydrocephalus).

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman is having EXTREME DIFFICULTY WALKING UP, and also finds it DIFFICULT TO STAND UP, saying her LEGS FEEL WEAK. She has a history of severe CHRONIC OBSTRUCTIVE PULMONARY DISEASE for which she has taken FREQUENT COURSES OF PREDNISOLONE.

choices.label: A

choices.label: D

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Spastic gait shows stiff, adducted, scissoring legs that cross with plantarflexed ankles - classic bilateral UMN/spastic diplegia (e.g. cerebral palsy).

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Waddling gait is caused by a proximal myopathy; chronic STEROID use causes a proximal (steroid) myopathy giving difficulty rising from a chair and a waddling gait.

questions.keyPoint: Long-term steroids cause a PROXIMAL myopathy - difficulty standing from a chair and a waddling gait.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man attends the emergency department experiencing SUDDEN-ONSET DIZZINESS earlier in the day. He also has problems ARTICULATING HIS WORDS (DYSARTHRIA) and SWALLOWING FOOD (DYSPHAGIA).

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Lacunar stroke: pure motor/sensory deficit from a small deep infarct, no cortical signs.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Partial anterior circulation stroke: 2 of (higher cortical dysfunction, homonymous hemianopia, motor/sensory deficit).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Posterior circulation stroke: brainstem/cerebellar signs - vertigo, dysarthria, dysphagia, diplopia, ataxia; this man has sudden DIZZINESS with DYSARTHRIA and DYSPHAGIA pointing to the brainstem.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Total anterior circulation stroke: all 3 of higher cortical dysfunction + hemianopia + motor/sensory deficit.

questions.keyPoint: Dizziness/vertigo with dysarthria, dysphagia or diplopia localises to the brainstem = posterior circulation stroke.

source.paper: Get Ahead Neuro EMQ · Page 6 | Theme 1: Classification of stroke

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with WEAKNESS IN HIS LEFT ARM. He also appears CONFUSED (HIGHER CORTICAL DYSFUNCTION), and his wife says this is new for him. On examination there is NO EVIDENCE OF VISUAL FIELD DEFICIT.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Lacunar stroke: pure motor/sensory deficit from a small deep infarct, no cortical signs.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Partial anterior circulation stroke: 2 of (higher cortical dysfunction, homonymous hemianopia, motor/sensory deficit).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Total anterior circulation stroke: all 3 of higher cortical dysfunction + hemianopia + motor/sensory deficit.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TIA: focal deficit fully resolving within 24 hours.

questions.keyPoint: 2 of the 3 Bamford criteria (here motor deficit + higher cortical dysfunction, no hemianopia) = partial anterior circulation stroke.

questions.stem: An 81-year-old woman is unable to get out of bed because she is WEAK IN HER RIGHT ARM AND LEG. You notice she has TROUBLE UNDERSTANDING what is said and cannot comprehend (DYSPHASIA / HIGHER CORTICAL DYSFUNCTION). On examination she is BLIND IN THE LATERAL VISUAL FIELD of the right eye (HOMONYMOUS HEMIANOPIA).

choices.label: C

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Left XII (hypoglossal) lesion: tongue wasting/fasciculation with deviation TO THE LEFT on protrusion (tongue points to the weak side).

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Left XI (accessory) lesion: weakness turning the head to the RIGHT (left SCM) and shrugging the LEFT shoulder (left trapezius).

choices.label: I

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: IX, X lesion: hoarse voice, dysphagia, palatal weakness with loss of gag reflex (glossopharyngeal + vagus).

questions.keyPoint: Tongue wasting/fasciculation with deviation TO the weak side = ipsilateral (here left) XII (hypoglossal) lesion.

choices.explanation: Drop attack: SUDDEN FALL TO THE GROUND WITHOUT LOSS OF CONSCIOUSNESS, typically in older women, with immediate recovery.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Anxiety attack: hyperventilation, paraesthesiae, carpopedal spasm.

questions.keyPoint: A faint on getting up, in a young woman, with the typical prodrome = vasovagal syncope (postural hypotension B is the main alternative).

questions.stem: A 75-year-old RIGHT-HANDED woman is noted to IGNORE STIMULI ON THE LEFT SIDE of her body (sensory inattention/neglect).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Medial longitudinal fasciculus lesion: internuclear ophthalmoplegia - failure of adduction with nystagmus of the abducting eye; bilateral INO is classic of MS.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Caudate nucleus: degeneration causes chorea (e.g. Huntington's disease) with dementia and a family history.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Dominant (usually left) temporal lobe: receptive (Wernicke's) dysphasia - fluent jargon speech that cannot be understood, with impaired comprehension.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Right (non-dominant) parietal lobe: contralateral sensory inattention/neglect - ignoring stimuli on the LEFT side of the body.

questions.keyPoint: Contralateral sensory neglect (ignoring the left side) = non-dominant (right) parietal lobe lesion.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman on antiepileptic therapy complains of ACNE and INCREASED FACIAL HAIR GROWTH (hirsutism). She is on no other medication.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Paraldehyde: given rectally in status epilepticus and characteristically causes RECTAL IRRITATION.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Sodium valproate: causes TREMOR, WEIGHT GAIN, HAIR THINNING/LOSS, HEPATOTOXICITY (raised liver enzymes); broad-spectrum.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Phenytoin: causes GUM HYPERTROPHY, HIRSUTISM, ACNE, coarse facies, ataxia and a rash.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Lamotrigine: can cause a serious RASH / STEVENS-JOHNSON SYNDROME, especially when introduced as add-on or titrated quickly.

questions.keyPoint: Acne, hirsutism and gum hypertrophy are classic phenytoin adverse effects.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with ISCHAEMIC HEART DISEASE requires treatment for an ACUTE MIGRAINE ATTACK THAT HAS NOT RESPONDED TO PARACETAMOL.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Paracetamol: simple analgesia, appropriate for mild post-traumatic headache under observation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Prednisolone: high-dose oral steroid - urgent treatment for giant cell arteritis to prevent blindness.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: High-dose aspirin and metoclopramide: acute migraine treatment of choice when triptans are CONTRAINDICATED (e.g. ischaemic heart disease).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Acetazolamide: carbonic anhydrase inhibitor - lowers intraocular pressure (acute glaucoma) and CSF production (IIH).

questions.keyPoint: In acute migraine where triptans are contraindicated (ischaemic heart disease), use high-dose aspirin with an antiemetic (metoclopramide).

questions.stem: IMPROVEMENT OF TREMOR in a 70-year-old patient with PARKINSON'S DISEASE on treatment with LEVODOPA.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Clozapine: atypical antipsychotic of choice for drug-induced psychosis in Parkinson's disease (does not worsen motor symptoms).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Bromocriptine: dopamine agonist - useful when levodopa is not tolerated.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Benzhexol (trihexyphenidyl): anticholinergic that improves TREMOR in Parkinson's.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Apomorphine: potent subcutaneous dopamine agonist for severe Parkinson's uncontrolled on maximal oral therapy.

questions.keyPoint: Anticholinergics (e.g. benzhexol) are useful for tremor-predominant Parkinson's disease.
